scope: executable async/scheduling/lifecycle/error-channel hard-negative fixture expansion; no product behavior or exact admission changes

after_summary.note: This issue does not claim every #655 matrix class now has a brand-new executable fixture. It maps all 48 classes to explicit evidence status, adds 54 direct assertions for 40 classes, and leaves 14 classes flagged for more granular future executable follow-up before broader exact admission.

jq -e '.parent_issue == 663 and .broader_context_issue == 653 and .hard_gate.semantic_admission_delta == 0 and .baseline_from_issue_655.expected_exact_admission_delta == 0 and .added_executable_coverage.assertions_added == 54 and .after_summary.hard_negative_fixture_groups_after == 28' bench/recall_loss/issue-657-hard-negative-fixtures-2026-07-02.v1.json

notes: This issue intentionally records executable fail-closed behavior instead of opening exact admission.

The new suite uses value-fingerprint hard negatives for runtime/protocol distinctions that the helper observes directly; unit-wrapper async/sync boundaries remain covered at query level because they are represented outside the value-fingerprint payload.

The first narrow run initially exposed that Python and Swift async-function wrappers are not visible to value_fingerprint alone; those assertions were kept out of the new equivalence suite and left to existing query-level fixtures.
